moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with the flexible Nf3, a hallmark of the King's Indian Attack system. This move controls e5 and d4, preparing a kingside fianchetto while keeping our options open—we can adapt to whatever Black plays. Unlike 1.e4 or 1.d4, this move is less committal and lets us steer the game into familiar territory regardless of Black's setup.

Black mirrors our development and contests the center's key squares. By playing Nf6, Black prevents us from easily controlling e5 and signals that they won't allow a quick pawn center. This is a solid, flexible response that keeps Black's position balanced.

Now we commit to the fianchetto, preparing to place our bishop on g2 where it will dominate the long diagonal. The g3 move supports our e5 plans and prevents Black from easily organizing pressure on d4—our bishop will quietly control the center from g2 without needing pawn support there.

Black immediately stakes a claim in the center with d5, which is the principled response to our setup. Rather than let us quietly solidify control on the long diagonal, Black forces the issue and creates immediate tension—now we must decide how to handle the d5 pawn.

Our bishop takes its ideal square on the long diagonal, eyeing c6 and pressing toward d5. From g2, the bishop is perfectly placed to support future pressure in the center while keeping our kingside safe. This is the soul of the King's Indian Attack: patient, prophylactic piece placement.

Black supports the d5 pawn and prepares to anchor their center, while also eyeing the d5 square for potential piece placement. The move c6 is solid and flexible—it keeps Black's structure intact and gives the d5 pawn needed support against our long-diagonal pressure.

We castle kingside to secure our king and connect our rooks, freeing up the f1 square for potential piece maneuvering. Castling also prepares us to shift our attention toward the center and queenside; with our king safe, we can pursue our plan of slow buildup without worry.

Black develops the bishop outside the pawn chain, avoiding the typical Be7 setup and keeping more flexibility. By placing the bishop on f5, Black controls e4 and eyes the long diagonal as well, setting up a more active middlegame compared to a quieter setup.

We support the e4 square and prepare to challenge Black's center when the moment is right. The d3 pawn is the backbone of our setup—it controls e4 and c4, and it allows us to eventually push e4 if we want to, or keep the position closed and maneuvering. It's patient but purposeful.

Black closes the center and stabilizes their position, signaling that they won't allow a quick e4 breakthrough. The e6 pawn supports d5 and prepares Be7 or Nbd7 development. Black is consolidating their solid pawn structure and preventing our natural expanding plans.

This is the key attacking idea—we reroute the knight to f5, where it will pressure Black's center and kingside from an aggressive square. Nh4 looks odd but it's precisely because our knight can't be easily challenged on f5, and from there it will eye g7 and e7, forcing Black to respond carefully. This prophylactic approach is typical of the King's Indian Attack's maneuvering style.
